---
title: "Security and Disclosure Controls"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Security and Disclosure Controls}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(eval = FALSE)
```

## Overview

dsOMOP v2 enforces DataSHIELD disclosure controls at every level. The controls
are self-contained -- dsOMOP does **not** depend on dsBase for disclosure
checking, which avoids version coupling. All thresholds are read from
DataSHIELD server options that the Opal administrator configures; the client can
only read these settings, never change them.

## The Per-Patient Gate

The central control is a **per-patient gate** keyed on the number of *unique
persons*, counted as `COUNT(DISTINCT person_id)` (or `subject_id` for cohorts).
Every result that leaves the server -- exploration aggregates, plan/recipe
extractions, cohort creation/combination/`from_table`, and the data-manipulation
verbs (`ds.omop.merge` / `filter` / `select` / `bind_rows`) -- flows through
`.assertMinPersons`, which compares the distinct-person count against
`nfilter.subset`.

The gate is **fail-closed**: if the count is below the floor (or `NA`), the
operation is blocked with a deliberately uninformative error that does not reveal
the actual count:

```
Error: Disclosive: operation blocked — insufficient individuals to meet
disclosure threshold. No further details available.
```

Because the gate counts *distinct persons* rather than rows, narrowing
operations (filters, set differences, joins) can only ever reduce the population,
and a frame that describes too few individuals is never created. Even
`ds.omop.bind_rows` cannot inflate a population past the floor by binding a frame
to itself, since duplicated persons do not raise the distinct count.

Aggregate counts that *survive* the gate are additionally **banded** down to a
multiple of a band width before being returned, so an exact supra-threshold count
cannot be used as a differencing primitive (this is what `ds.omop.plan.preview()`
reports as `n_persons`).

## The Person Token

Row-level person and subject keys never leave the server as raw ids. Before any
value is assigned into the DataSHIELD session, each id is replaced -- under its
original column name -- by a pseudonymous **person token** with four properties:

- **Persistent / deterministic.** The token is the AES-256-CBC encryption of the
  id under a per-resource secret key (plus a derived fixed IV), so the same id
  always maps to the same token. Tokens are stable across reconnects and across
  DataSHIELD workspace save/load, and remain joinable on the key.
- **Per-resource.** A different resource uses a different key, so the same person
  is *not* linkable across sites by comparing tokens.
- **Non-numeric.** Each token is `paste0("p", <hex ciphertext>)`. The leading
  `"p"` guarantees the token is non-numeric: hex alone could be all digits, which
  `as.numeric()` / `ds.asNumeric()` would parse back into a number; the letter
  prefix forces those to `NA`. The id therefore cannot be recovered or inferred
  client-side, even by coercion.
- **Reversible server-side only.** The transform is invertible *on the server*
  (used for population-scoping joins, e.g. `ds.omop.cohort.from_table()`), but
  the **client never holds the key** and so cannot invert a token.

The token column is also tagged `dsomop_protected`, so the factor/level layer and
the manipulation verbs refuse to expose it, use it as a grouping/stat variable,
or let it be filtered on. Every other identifier column is dropped outright.

This pseudonymisation is defense-in-depth, **not** the sole protection: the
authoritative barrier remains output gating (the per-patient gate plus small-cell
suppression). A high-cardinality token is safe to retain precisely because it can
never be a stat/group variable and can never be reversed off-server.

## Disclosure Settings

Each setting is read with a `getOption()` chain: the direct option, then a
`default.*` fallback (declared in the package `DATASHIELD` file), then a
hard-coded default.

### nfilter.subset (minimum persons)

The per-patient floor described above. Default `3`. Enforced on
`COUNT(DISTINCT person_id)` for every result path.

### nfilter.tab (minimum cell count)

Minimum count for frequency distributions; smaller cells are suppressed.
Default `3`. Affects `ds.omop.value.counts()`, `ds.omop.crosstab()`, table/column
statistics, and prevalence.

### nfilter.levels.max (maximum factor levels)

Maximum number of distinct values allowed for a categorical column, preventing
extraction of high-cardinality identifiers. Default `40`.

### nfilter.levels.density

Maximum ratio of distinct levels to observations, blocking near-unique columns.
Default `0.33`.

### nfilter.string / nfilter.stringShort / nfilter.noise

Standard DataSHIELD string-length and noise parameters (defaults `80`, `20`,
`0.25`).

### dsOMOP-specific settings

- `dsomop.query_strict` (default `TRUE`): keeps the ad-hoc query library in
  strict, fully-gated mode.
- `dsomop.nfilter.dist` (default `10`): minimum support for distribution-style
  outputs.
- `dsomop.allow_absolute_dates` (default `FALSE`, **locked**): when off, raw
  calendar event dates are removed from outputs; the admin must explicitly opt
  in.
- `dsomop.allow_sensitive_columns` (default `FALSE`, **locked**): gates extraction
  of columns flagged sensitive.

## Where Controls Are Enforced

| Operation | Gate |
|---|---|
| Exploration aggregates | `nfilter.subset` on distinct persons; `nfilter.tab` cell suppression; `nfilter.levels.*` on categoricals |
| Plan / recipe execution | each output gated on distinct persons; concept factors built from disclosure-safe levels |
| Plan / recipe preview | per-output `disclosive` flag; surviving counts banded; no data created |
| Cohort create / combine / from_table | `nfilter.subset` on the resulting cohort |
| Manipulation verbs (merge/filter/select/bind_rows) | result re-gated on distinct person tokens, re-tagged `omop.table`, fail-closed |
| Row-level filter DSL | range/membership/`value_bin` only; exact-match `==`/`!=` and identifier columns rejected; gate still runs on the filtered result |

## SQL Safety

dsOMOP never builds SQL from raw client strings. Table and column names are
validated against `^[a-zA-Z_][a-zA-Z0-9_]*$` and quoted as identifiers, literal
values are quoted, concept ids are cast to integer, and the filter DSL is
compiled recursively with an operator whitelist.

## Server Administrator Guide

The Opal administrator sets the thresholds via DataSHIELD options. Recommended
hardened settings:

```r
options(
  nfilter.subset         = 5,    # minimum unique persons (the per-patient gate)
  nfilter.tab            = 5,    # minimum cell count
  nfilter.levels.max     = 40,   # max categorical levels
  nfilter.levels.density = 0.33  # max level density
)
```

### The DATASHIELD file

The package `DATASHIELD` file declares each method and its `default.*` option, so
Opal applies sane defaults at install time. These are the fallbacks used when a
direct option is unset:

```
Options:
  default.nfilter.subset=3
  default.nfilter.tab=3
  default.nfilter.levels.max=40
  default.nfilter.levels.density=0.33
  default.dsomop.query_strict=TRUE
  default.dsomop.allow_absolute_dates=FALSE
  default.dsomop.allow_sensitive_columns=FALSE
```

The administrator overrides any of these in the Opal admin panel. The opt-out
permissions (`allow_absolute_dates`, `allow_sensitive_columns`) are locked off by
default and must be enabled deliberately. None of this is reachable from the
client, which can only observe the effective behaviour.
